did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model to classify eight types of skin diseases (Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Rosacea, Nevus, Melanoma, Eczema, Actinic Keratosis, and Seborrheic Keratosis) from skin images. The model was trained on a dataset of 2800 images (including data from ISIC) and achieved 95% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin disease detection. It details specific methodology including data collection (2800 images from ISIC and other sources), preprocessing, architecture design (6 convolutional layers, 218,072 parameters), and training (500 epochs). It reports original results, including 95% accuracy and AUC values, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Detection With Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for the automated detection and classification of eight common skin diseases, including Acne, Melanoma, and Basal Cell Carcinoma. The model was trained on a dataset of 2,800 images, with five disease types sourced from the ISIC dataset and others from reliable external resources. Data preprocessing involved resizing images to 256x256 pixels and applying augmentation techniques such as flipping and contrast adjustment to enhance robustness. The custom architecture consists of six convolutional layers, max pooling layers, and two dense layers, totaling 218,072 trainable parameters. The system was evaluated using accuracy and Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) metric. The model achieved a final accuracy of 95% on the test set, with perfect AUC scores (1.00) for Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, and Rosacea, demonstrating high discriminatory power.

all_task_types.target: Eight discrete skin disease categories (Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Rosacea, Nevus, Melanoma, Eczema, Actinic Keratosis, and Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin diseases using a custom CNN with softmax activation for probability distribution over 8 classes. Achieved 95% accuracy on the test set. AUC scores reported for each class (1.00 for Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Rosacea; 0.99 for Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis; 0.97 for Nevus; 0.95 for Melanoma).

datasets_used.description: A public dataset used to source images for five of the eight skin disease classes studied in the research.

use.validation: Part of the combined dataset used for validation.
use.testing: Part of the combined dataset used for testing.

datasets_used.name: Combined Custom Dataset
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset created by the authors consisting of 2800 total images of eight skin diseases, split into training, validation, and testing sets.

use.training: Used to train the custom CNN model for 500 epochs.
use.validation: Used to validate the model in each iteration during training.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance, achieving 95% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between positive and negative cases for each specific disease class and to calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) as an indicator of discriminatory power.

To quantify the performance of the binary classifier for each of the eight skin disease classes, indicating the probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen positive instance higher than a randomly chosen negative one.
To measure the overall proportion of correct predictions (true positives and true negatives) out of the total number of cases in the test dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics.

Combined Custom Dataset (2800 images).notes: The dataset was split into Training Set (80%, 2240 images), Validation Set (10%, 280 images), and Test Set (10%, 280 images). The model was trained for 500 epochs. AUC values were also reported for individual classes: Acne (1.00), Basal Cell Carcinoma (1.00), Eczema (1.00), Rosacea (1.00), Actinic Keratosis (0.99), Seborrheic Keratosis (0.99), Nevus (0.97), and Melanoma (0.95).

architecture_used.pretraining: None (custom lightweight model, not a pre-trained heavy-weight model)

architecture_details.layers: 6 convolutional layers, each followed by a max pooling layer; 1 flattening layer; 2 dense layers
architecture_details.activation: Softmax activation function used in the final classification layer

training_details.loss_function: Not reported (Softmax implies categorical cross-entropy for multi-class, but not explicitly stated)
training_details.hyperparameters: Hyperparameters were fine-tuned to optimize performance (specific values not reported)
training_details.data_augmentation: Vertically flipped, upscaled contrast, different color grading

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform size of 256x256 pixels
Image augmentation using Tensorflow to generate variations: vertical flipping, upscaled contrast, and different color grading

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were vertically flipped using the image augmentation feature of Tensorflow to generate variations.
Upscaled contrast was applied to images to enhance robustness.
Different color grading was applied to images to generate variations.

conclusions: The proposed custom lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model successfully detects eight types of skin diseases (Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Rosacea, Nevus, Melanoma, Eczema, Actinic Keratosis, and Seborrheic Keratosis) with an overall accuracy of 95% on the test dataset.
The model demonstrated exceptional discriminatory power for Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, and Rosacea, achieving perfect Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores of 1.00.

High AUC values were observed for Actinic Keratosis (0.99), Seborrheic Keratosis (0.99), Nevus (0.97), and Melanoma (0.95), indicating the model's robustness in differentiating between diseased and healthy samples.

The system has the potential to aid dermatologists in clinical settings by enabling timely detection and treatment, reducing misdiagnosis rates, and improving patient outcomes through automated triage.
The technology offers significant promise for cost savings and improved accessibility to dermatological care, particularly in underserved areas where dermatologists are scarce.
The study acknowledges that early detection and accurate diagnosis can alleviate patient anxiety and enhance psychological well-being.
important_remarks: The dataset is small (2,800 images total) and heavily relies on the ISIC dataset for five of the eight classes, while the remaining three classes (Acne, Rosacea, Eczema) are sourced from unspecified 'reliable resources,' raising concerns about data provenance and potential bias.

The paper reports perfect AUC scores (1.00) for four classes (Acne, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Eczema, Rosacea), which is unusually high for medical image classification and may indicate data leakage, insufficient class separation in the small dataset, or overfitting.
Critical hyperparameters such as the optimizer, learning rate, and loss function are not explicitly reported, limiting reproducibility.
The study lacks external validation on independent, diverse datasets, relying solely on an internal 80/10/10 split of a proprietary combined dataset.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) is provided to support the reported accuracy and AUC metrics.
The model was trained for 500 epochs with fine-tuned hyperparameters, but there is no evidence provided that this did not lead to overfitting, especially given the small dataset size.
The paper does not address potential skin tone bias or demographic diversity of the subjects in the ISIC and external datasets, which is a critical limitation for clinical generalizability.
